figures.caption: Table 3: Breakdown of performance across four evaluation dimensions for critic-in-the-loop refinement cycles and the two baselines, using the Distill-Qwen-14B model. Percentages indicate relative change compared to the initial code baseline.

Table 2: Performance comparison across different generator models and critic-in-the-loop cycles, as measured by the multidimensional VLM judge. Also shown is the performance of refinement without a critic and the performance of the Distill-Qwen-14B model after LoRA fine-tuning. The best solution obtained during critic in the loop is marked by “CITL: Best”.
Table 4: Component score by cycle for the Haiku 4.5 and Sonnet 4.5 generator models.

Figure 1: Left: Overview of the critic-in-the-loop (CITL) pipeline for web code generation. Right (top): Overall score versus token usage, illustrating the quality–efficiency trade-off across refinement cycles. Right (bottom): Breakdown of VLM-judged performance across four evaluation dimensions. Performance figures are based on the Distill-Qwen-14B generator model.

Figure 3: Distribution of critique categories. Visual Polish: spacing, alignment, refinement. Missing Elements: incomplete sections or functionality. Implementation: code quality, accessibility issues. Content: hierarchy and organization. Interactivity: hover states, feedback. Responsive: mobile adaptation. Components: UI element consistency. Typography: fonts, sizing, readability.
Figure 2: Notation used in the paper (left) alongside the Critic-in-the-Loop (CITL) algorithm (right).
